grammar_points.title: 〜させられる (Causative-Passive)
grammar_points.description: 〜させられる combines the causative (make/let) and passive (be done to) forms to mean you were forced to do something — usually against your will, with a nuance of reluctance or inconvenience.

grammar_points.title: 〜てしまう・〜ちゃう
grammar_points.description: 〜てしまう attaches to the て form to show an action is completely finished, or that it happened with a sense of regret — "ended up doing." 〜ちゃう・〜じゃう are the casual contractions.

grammar_points.title: 〜なければならない・〜なきゃ
grammar_points.description: 〜なければならない expresses obligation — "must do" or "have to do." It literally means "if you don't do it, it won't do," and 〜なきゃ is the everyday casual contraction.

grammar_points.title: 〜てはいけない・〜ちゃだめ
grammar_points.description: 〜てはいけない attaches to the て form to express prohibition — "must not do" / "you're not allowed to." 〜ちゃだめ and 〜ちゃいけない are the casual equivalents.

grammar_points.title: 〜なくてもいい
grammar_points.description: 〜なくてもいい means "don't have to" / "there's no need to." It's the negative counterpart of obligation — telling someone an action isn't necessary.

grammar_points.title: 〜べきだ
grammar_points.description: 〜べきだ expresses that someone should or ought to do something — a strong opinion, moral duty, or firm piece of advice about the right thing to do.

grammar_points.title: 〜そうだ (Hearsay)
grammar_points.description: The hearsay 〜そうだ reports information you heard or read from another source — "I hear that…" / "they say that…" It attaches to the plain form and is often paired with 〜によると ("according to").

grammar_points.title: 〜らしい
grammar_points.description: 〜らしい means "seems like" or "apparently" — a conjecture based on information you've gathered from outside (hearsay, reports, reputation). It can also work as a suffix meaning "typical of" or "-like."

grammar_points.title: 〜ようだ・〜みたいだ
grammar_points.description: 〜ようだ and 〜みたいだ both mean "it seems / it appears," a conjecture drawn from your own observation. They also form similes — "like a…" — with 〜みたいだ being the casual spoken version of the more formal 〜ようだ.

grammar_points.title: 〜ため（に）
grammar_points.description: 〜ため（に）marks a purpose ("in order to"), a beneficiary ("for the sake of"), or a cause ("because of / due to") depending on context. It is more formal than 〜から・〜ので and is common in written Japanese.

grammar_points.title: 〜せいで
grammar_points.description: 〜せいで attributes a bad or unwanted outcome to a cause — it assigns blame. "Because of / thanks to (negatively)." Attach it to a noun with の or to a plain-form clause.

grammar_points.title: 〜おかげで
grammar_points.description: 〜おかげで credits a good outcome to a cause — "thanks to." It is the positive counterpart of 〜せいで. Attach it to a noun with の or to a plain-form clause.

grammar_points.title: 〜ても (Even If)
grammar_points.description: 〜ても attaches to the て form to mean "even if" or "even though" — the first action or condition does not prevent the second. It is the neutral concessive: no blame, no frustration, just "A, but B happens anyway."

grammar_points.title: 〜くせに
grammar_points.description: 〜くせに means "even though," but unlike neutral concessives it always carries blame, criticism, or contempt toward the subject. Use it to point out that someone acts in a way that clashes with what they are or know.

grammar_points.title: 〜にもかかわらず
grammar_points.description: 〜にもかかわらず means "despite" or "in spite of." It is a formal, written-sounding concessive: something happens even though the situation would normally prevent or discourage it.

grammar_points.title: 〜ことにする・〜ことになる
grammar_points.description: 〜ことにする is a decision you make yourself ("I've decided to"), while 〜ことになる is a decision or outcome that comes about through circumstances ("it's been decided that"). The related 〜ことにしている and 〜ことになっている express personal habits and standing rules.

grammar_points.title: 〜ばかり
grammar_points.description: 〜ばかり has three main jobs: with the た form it means "just did (something a moment ago)"; after a noun it means "only / nothing but"; and with the て form (〜てばかりいる) it means "keeps doing nothing but." Context and what it attaches to tell you which meaning is in play.

grammar_points.title: 〜ところだ
grammar_points.description: 〜ところだ pins an action to a point in time. The form of the verb in front of ところ sets the moment: dictionary form = about to do it, 〜ている form = right in the middle of it, and た form = just finished it.

grammar_points.title: 〜わけだ・〜わけではない
grammar_points.description: 〜わけだ means "so that's why / no wonder" — a conclusion that makes sense once you learn a new fact. Its negative 〜わけではない means "it's not that / it doesn't mean that," softening or partially denying a claim.

grammar_points.title: 〜はずだ・〜はずがない
grammar_points.description: 〜はずだ expresses a logical expectation — "it should be (so)," based on reasoning or known facts. Its counterpart 〜はずがない means "there's no way / it can't possibly be," a confident denial of a possibility.

grammar_points.title: 〜に違いない
grammar_points.description: 〜に違（ちが）いない means "must be" or "no doubt" — a strong, confident inference. You're not stating a proven fact, but you're sure enough about it that there's "no mistaking" the conclusion.

grammar_points.title: 〜ようになる・〜ようにする
grammar_points.description: 〜ようになる marks a change — coming to a point where something is possible or habitual ("came to be able to"). 〜ようにする is about deliberate effort — making sure something happens ("try to / make a point of"). A bare verb ＋ ように also means "so that."

grammar_points.title: 〜ようとする・〜ようと思う
grammar_points.description: Both build on the volitional form (〜よう). 〜ようとする means "to be about to / try to" — you're on the verge of an action or making an attempt. 〜ようと思（おも）う means "I'm thinking of doing" — a softer statement of intention.

grammar_points.title: 〜について
grammar_points.description: 〜について attaches to a noun to mean "about" or "concerning" — it marks the topic you are talking, thinking, writing, or asking about.

grammar_points.title: 〜による・〜によって・〜によると
grammar_points.description: 〜による and 〜によって attach to a noun to mean "depending on," "by means of," or "due to," while 〜によると introduces a source for hearsay — "according to ..." — and usually pairs with 〜そうだ.

grammar_points.title: 〜にとって
grammar_points.description: 〜にとって attaches to a noun (usually a person or group) to mean "for ~" or "from ~'s point of view" — it frames how something looks or matters from someone's perspective.

grammar_points.title: 〜として
grammar_points.description: 〜として attaches to a noun to mean "as ~" — it states the role, capacity, status, or function in which someone or something acts or is treated.

grammar_points.title: 〜にしては
grammar_points.description: 〜にしては means "for ~" or "considering it's ~" — it sets up a standard, then points out that reality is surprisingly different from what that standard would lead you to expect.

grammar_points.title: 〜かわりに
grammar_points.description: 〜かわりに (代（か）わりに) means "instead of ~" or "in exchange for ~" — it swaps one thing for another, or balances one thing against a trade-off.

grammar_points.title: しか〜ない
grammar_points.description: しか is a particle meaning "only" that must be paired with a negative verb. しか〜ない limits something to a single option and carries the nuance that it isn't much or isn't enough.

grammar_points.title: 〜まま
grammar_points.description: 〜まま means "as is" or "leaving something in an unchanged state" — an action or condition stays the way it was while something else happens.

grammar_points.title: 〜ばよかった
grammar_points.description: 〜ばよかった expresses regret about the past — "I wish I had ~" or "I should have ~." It combines the ば conditional form with よかった ("it would have been good").

grammar_points.title: 〜といい
grammar_points.description: 〜といい expresses a hope or wish — "I hope ~" or "it'd be good if ~." The と is the conditional "if," so literally it's "if ~ happens, that would be good."

grammar_points.title: 〜はじめる・〜おわる
grammar_points.description: Attach 始（はじ）める or 終（お）わる to a verb's ますstem to make a compound verb meaning "start doing ~" or "finish doing ~."

grammar_points.title: 〜たて
grammar_points.description: 〜たて attaches to a verb stem to mean "freshly ~" or "just-~ed" — an action finished only moments ago, with the result still fresh. Common with cooking and other made-just-now things.

grammar_points.title: 〜そうになる
grammar_points.description: 〜そうになる attaches to a verb stem to mean "almost did ~" or "was about to ~" — something nearly happened but didn't. Usually used in the past as 〜そうになった.

grammar_points.title: 〜ほど〜ない
grammar_points.description: The 〜ほど〜ない pattern compares two things and says one is "not as ~ as" the other. The noun before ほど is the standard you fall short of.

grammar_points.title: 〜たほうがいい・〜ないほうがいい
grammar_points.description: 〜ほうがいい gives advice or states a preference — "it's better to ~." Use the past た form to recommend doing something, and the plain ない form to recommend not doing it.

grammar_points.title: 〜ごとに
grammar_points.description: 〜ごとに attaches to a noun to mean "every ~" or "at each ~" — marking regular intervals or that something applies to each member of a group.

grammar_points.title: 〜方（かた）
grammar_points.description: 〜方（かた） attaches to a verb stem to mean "way of doing ~" or "how to ~." The result is a noun: 使（つか）い方（かた） = way of using, how to use.

grammar_points.title: せっかく
grammar_points.description: せっかく is an adverb marking that effort, trouble, or a rare opportunity went into something — "with great effort," "went to the trouble of," or "long-awaited." It often carries a sense that this effort shouldn't go to waste.

grammar_points.title: 〜とか〜とか
grammar_points.description: 〜とか〜とか lists examples in a casual, non-exhaustive way — "things like A and B." It implies there are other unlisted items too.

grammar_points.title: 〜以上・〜以外
grammar_points.description: Two similar-looking suffixes: 〜以上（いじょう） means "more than / at least / above," while 〜以外（いがい） means "except / other than." Different kanji, different meanings — don't mix them up.

grammar_points.title: 〜向け
grammar_points.description: 〜向（む）け attaches to a noun to mean "aimed at" or "intended for" a particular group or audience — 子供（こども）向（む）け = for children.
